---
title: "Methodology"
---

## Why Forecast Attendance?

Stadium attendance is a leading indicator for sports business operations:
- **Revenue planning** for concessions, parking, and hospitality
- **Broadcast scheduling** and commentary crew sizing
- **Event operations** (parking, security, catering)
- **Research**: Understand fan behavior, team performance correlation, seasonal patterns

Modern soccer analytics relies on quantitative forecasting, yet attendance is surprisingly under-studied compared to match outcomes or player performance.

---

## Data Source

- **API**: [football-data.org](https://www.football-data.org/) (free tier)
- **League**: Premier League (20 teams, 38 matchdays per season)
- **History**: 2020–21 through 2024–25 seasons (~1,900 matches)
- **Limitation**: Free tier doesn't always include actual attendance; we estimate using stadium capacity (70% utilization baseline)

This is a deliberate design choice documented transparently—most real-world ML projects work with imperfect data. The important thing is to acknowledge limitations and validate model performance despite them.

---

## Feature Engineering

14 hand-crafted features capture team form, fixture prestige, and temporal factors:

| Feature | Type | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| `home_win_rate_last5` | % | Recent form is predictive of momentum and crowd confidence |
| `away_win_rate_last5` | % | Away teams' recent success attracts traveling fans |
| `home_goals_scored_avg` | # | Attacking teams draw larger crowds (entertaining football) |
| `away_goals_conceded_avg` | # | Defensive vulnerability = more goals = more excitement |
| `is_rivalry` | bool | Historic rivalries (Man Utd vs Man City, Arsenal vs Spurs) are high-draw events |
| `matchday` | int | Late-season matches (playoffs) have higher urgency |
| `days_since_last_home_game` | # | Rest/fatigue affects team performance visibility |
| `season_progress` | 0-1 | Seasonal trend (opening vs. closing matches) |
| `home_league_position` | 1-20 | Top-of-table matches attract more spectators |
| `away_league_position` | 1-20 | Away team quality matters for appeal |
| `position_gap` | # | Mismatches (1st vs. 20th) draw different crowds |
| `is_weekend` | bool | Saturday/Sunday matches > midweek matches |
| `month` | int | Seasonal variation (holidays, weather) |
| `stadium_capacity` | # | Normalize by ground size |

All features are engineered **before** match time (no data leakage).

---

## Model: LightGBM

**Why LightGBM over alternatives?**

- **ARIMA / Prophet**: Assumption of linear trend doesn't hold for attendance (multimodal, regime changes)
- **Neural Networks**: Overkill for 1,900 training samples; LightGBM trains faster and is more interpretable
- **LightGBM**: Gradient boosting handles non-linear feature interactions (e.g., rivalry + big matchday), trains quickly, produces feature importances

**Hyperparameters**:
```python
n_estimators=500, learning_rate=0.05, max_depth=6,
subsample=0.8, colsample_bytree=0.8
```

---

## Backtesting Methodology

**Rolling-origin evaluation** (no data leakage):
- Train on seasons 2020, 2021 → Test on 2022
- Train on seasons 2020, 2021, 2022 → Test on 2023
- Train on seasons 2020, 2021, 2022, 2023 → Test on 2024

This simulates real-world usage where we train on historical data and predict the future.

**Metric: MAPE** (Mean Absolute Percentage Error)
- More interpretable than RMSE for business stakeholders ("off by 10%")
- Threshold: < 15% (reasonable for a difficult forecasting task)

---

## Known Limitations & Future Work

### Known Limitations
1. **Attendance data**: Free tier doesn't always report actual attendance; we use synthetic estimates
2. **External factors**: COVID lockdowns (2020–21), fan boycotts, weather, injuries—not captured
3. **Causality**: Features are correlates, not causes (e.g., large crowds enable big spending, not vice versa)
4. **Temporal autocorrelation**: Matches aren't independent; seasonal patterns repeat

### Future Improvements
- **Injuries** via official team sheets (FBref API or web scraping)
- **Weather** from Open-Meteo API (temperature, precipitation)
- **Fixture congestion** (domestic cups, European competition)
- **Expected goals (xG)** to proxy attacking quality
- **Sentiment** from Twitter/X (NLP on match previews)
- **Quantile regression** for tighter prediction intervals

---

## How to Run Locally

```bash
# Clone
git clone https://github.com/madebylemon/sports-attendance-forecast
cd sports-attendance-forecast

# Setup
pip install -r requirements.txt
echo "FOOTBALL_DATA_API_KEY=your_key_here" > .env

# Backfill data (~2 min)
python -m pipeline.backfill

# Engineer + train + forecast
make pipeline

# View dashboard
quarto preview dashboard/
```

Or use Docker:
```bash
docker compose -f docker/docker-compose.yml build
docker compose -f docker/docker-compose.yml run pipeline bash
python -m pipeline.backfill && make pipeline
```

---

## Architecture

```
API (football-data.org)
    ↓ [backfill + refresh]
Raw CSV (data/raw/)
    ↓ [feature engineering]
Parquet (data/processed/features.parquet)
    ↓ [train]
LightGBM model (data/processed/model.pkl) + MLflow runs
    ↓ [forecast]
Forecasts (data/processed/forecasts.parquet)
    ↓ [Quarto render]
Dashboard (GitHub Pages)
```

GitHub Actions orchestrates daily refreshes at 7am UTC. MLflow tracks all experiments in `mlruns/`.

---

## Questions?

- **Issues**: [GitHub Issues](https://github.com/madebylemon/sports-attendance-forecast/issues)
- **Contributing**: Contributions welcome! See README for contribution guidelines.
- **Citation**: If you use this project in research, cite the repository (BibTeX below):

```bibtex
@software{sports_attendance_2025,
  author = {madebylemon},
  title = {Sports Attendance Forecast: End-to-End MLOps Pipeline},
  year = {2025},
  url = {https://github.com/madebylemon/sports-attendance-forecast}
}
```
